title: CADENet: Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-Stream Enhancement Network for Adverse Weather Perception in Autonomous Driving
abstract: 恶劣天气（雨、雾、沙尘、雪）会降低自动驾驶车辆基于摄像头的物体检测性能。现有的“先增强后检测”方法会中断安全关键检测循环，违反硬实时要求。该问题的研究进展还受到一个未被充分认识的评估上限的制约：在退化图像上标注的真实值无法奖励那些恢复出标注者自身都未能识别物体的检测器，因此真正有效的图像增强可能只能带来接近平坦的F1值增益。本文提出CADENet（条件自适应异步双流增强网络），这是一个无需训练的三线程系统：线程S（YOLOv11n）以全帧率提供零附加延迟的检测结果；线程Q应用条件自适应增强（CAPE）并通过熵引导非极大值抑制（EG-NMS）融合结果，且不阻塞线程S；线程E提供CLIP零样本天气分类，因此新天气类别仅需新文本提示，无需标注数据和重新训练。在1327张DAWN图像上评估（YOLOv11m，IoU=0.5，置信度=0.25），CADENet在雪天场景下实现召回率=0.0103（微平均）、F1=0.0230，雨天场景下F1=0.0038。我们正式定义了DAWN类数据上的标注完整性偏差，因此报告的F1值是对真实增益的下界估计；召回率是免疫标注缺口的主要指标。无论增强负载如何，线程S可持续维持约44 FPS。本方法无需模型重训练或额外传感器硬件。

CADENet: Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-Stream Enhancement Network for Adverse Weather Perception in Autonomous Driving
Sherif Khairy
1,2
, and Catherine M. Elias
1
,
2
​
{}^{1,2\href https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1444-9816\,}
*This work was not supported by any organization
1
Computer Science & Engineering Department, German University in Cairo (GUC), Egypt
Sherif.ammen@gmail.com, catherine.elias@ieee.org
2
C-DRiVeS Lab: Cognitive Driving Research in Vehicular Systems, Cairo, Egypt
cdrives.researchlab@gmail.com
Abstract
Adverse weather—rain, fog, sand, and snow—degrades camera-based object detection in autonomous vehicles. Existing enhancement-then-detect approaches stall the safety-critical perception loop, violating hard real-time requirements. Progress on this problem is also constrained by an under-recognised
evaluation ceiling
: ground truth annotated on degraded images cannot credit a detector that recovers objects the annotators themselves could not see, so a genuinely useful enhancement can register as a near-flat
F
1
F_{1}
gain. The paper presents
CADENet
(Condition-Adaptive asynchronous Dual-stream Enhancement Network), a training-free three-thread system: Thread S (YOLOv11n) delivers detections at full frame rate with
zero added latency
; Thread Q applies condition-adaptive enhancement (CAPE) and fuses results via entropy-guided NMS (EG-NMS) without blocking Thread S; Thread E provides
CLIP zero-shot weather classification
, so
new weather categories require only a new text prompt
—no labelled data, no retraining. Evaluated on 1,327 DAWN images (YOLOv11m, IoU@0.5, conf = 0.25), CADENet achieves
Δ
​
Recall
=
+
0.0103
\Delta\text{Recall}\!=\!{+}0.0103
(micro),
Δ
​
F
1
=
+
0.0230
\Delta F_{1}\!=\!{+}0.0230
on snow and
Δ
​
F
1
=
+
0.0038
\Delta F_{1}\!=\!{+}0.0038
on rain. We formalise the annotation completeness bias on DAWN-class data, so the reported
F
1
F_{1}
values are
lower bounds
on the true gain; recall is the annotation-gap-immune headline metric. Thread S sustains
∼
44
{\sim}44
FPS regardless of enhancement load. No model retraining or additional sensor hardware is required.
I
Introduction
Camera-based object detection is the dominant perception modality in
autonomous vehicles, but degrades under adverse weather: rain streaks
corrupt feature maps, fog attenuates edges, sand scatters light, and snow introduces specular patches—each reducing recall and elevating
false-positive rates.
Prior work addresses weather robustness along three main axes.
Data augmentation
[
10
]
retrains
detectors on synthetic corruptions, but requires large retraining cost
and degrades on unseen severity levels.
Restoration-then-detection
[
8
,
11
,
16
]
cascades a restoration network with a standard detector,
achieving strong benchmark numbers at the expense of paired training
data and a single blocking pipeline.
Multi-modal sensor fusion
[
2
]
fuses
LiDAR, radar, and cameras but demands calibrated multi-sensor rigs.
The deployment gap.
All three axes block the safety-critical output path for 90–160 ms,
violating hard real-time requirements—a constraint unmodelled by
IA-YOLO
[
8
]
, DENet
[
11
]
, and
AWD-YOLO
[
16
]
.
Our approach.
CADENet
separates the safety path (Thread S, YOLOv11n,
∼
44
{\sim}44
FPS,
zero added latency
) from a parallel
quality-enhancement path (Thread Q: WEM
→
\to
CAPE
→
\to
YOLOv11m
→
\to
EG-NMS)
and a background analytics thread (Thread E: CLIP zero-shot weather
classification
[
12
]
+ ResNet50
k
k

recommendation).

5
]
was annotated on degraded images;
post-enhancement detections of newly visible objects are penalised as FP,
so
reported
Δ
​
F
1
\Delta F_{1}
is a lower bound on true gain
;
recall is annotation-gap-immune (formal treatment:
Section
V-B
).

II
Related Work
II-A
Physics-Based Image Restoration
He
et al.
[
3
]
established the
Dark Channel Prior
(DCP): at least one colour channel is near-zero in any clear patch,
while haze raises the minimum intensity.
DCP fails under artificial light sources where lamp returns contaminate
atmospheric light
A

(observed: fog

but require paired training data.

<
30

Yang
et al.
[
15
]
introduced deep joint rain detection-removal
at the expense of synthetic training data;
CADENet’s CAPE uses a training-free 5-stage morphological derain.
Snow and sand share a common structure: reflective or scattering
particulates reduce local contrast.
DesnowNet
[
9
]

[
14

II-B
Joint Enhancement and Detection
IA-YOLO
[
8
]
introduced a differentiable image
processing (DIP) module whose parameters are predicted by a small CNN,
jointly optimised end-to-end with YOLO.
DENet
[
11
]
cascades a U-Net restoration network with
detection under joint supervision.
AWD-YOLO
[
16
]
—the strongest published DAWN
baseline—adds a Faster Local-Region Self-Attention (FLRSA) module to
YOLOv11, reporting mAP@0.5 = 67.7% on DAWN.
Shared limitation
: all present
single-thread blocking
pipelines
—the safety-critical output path stalls during enhancement.
CADENet is the first to formalise the safety/quality separation as an
explicit architectural primitive for single-camera AV systems.
II-C
Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion and Entropy Gating
Bijelic
et al.
[
2
]
compute per-sensor
signal-quality entropy to gate fusion between gated NIR, LiDAR, radar,
and colour camera under dense fog.
This directly motivates PEE and EG-NMS:

is the single-camera

Sand and Snow: CLAHE
Both conditions attenuate local contrast via scattering.
CLAHE with clip = 2.0 applied to the LAB L channel compensates
without hue distortion, requiring no training data.

IV
Experimental Setup
IV-A
Dataset and Protocol
We evaluate on the DAWN dataset
[
5
]
, the standard
adverse-weather benchmark for YOLO-class detection systems
[
16
]
.
We use all DAWN images with valid Pascal VOC annotations:
fog 600, rain 200, sand 323, snow 204 — totalling 1,327 images
(1,325 with parseable GT). Fog comprises 45% of the set, reflecting
DAWN’s sampling distribution and dominating the micro-average.
All runs use YOLOv11m, confidence threshold = 0.25, IoU@0.5.
We distinguish

CUDA 12.6), a 12th-Gen Intel Core i5-12450H (8 cores), 8 GB RAM,
running Windows 11.
The benchmark pipeline ran sequentially (single-threaded) for reproducibility.
All component latencies were measured directly on the test hardware

I
).
Overall accuracy is 40.4%, with strong performance on snow (97.1%)
but low accuracy on fog (8.7%) and rain (20.5%), caused by WEM’s
LAB-space snow-bias under uniform-grey degraded scenes.
CLIP disambiguation (Module 6a) targets these failure cases, motivating
Thread E’s zero-cost high-accuracy classification.

V-B
Annotation Completeness Bias: CADENet Surpasses Ground Truth
DAWN GT was annotated on weather-degraded originals.
After enhancement, the detector correctly identifies real objects that
were too occluded to annotate under the original degradation.
This means
CADENet’s C2 detections surpass the completeness of
the DAWN ground truth itself
—the enhanced detector sees through
degradation that the human annotators could not.
Under COCO-style evaluation, these valid detections of
real but
unannotated
objects are counted as false positives, creating a
one-directional asymmetric bias
: C2 precision and
F
1
F_{1}
are
systematically suppressed relative to C1 even when the enhanced detector
is strictly correct, while C1 is unaffected:
Δ
​

Δ

records that CAPE-enhanced YOLO recovers 101 additional
annotated
GT objects that vanilla YOLO misses—uncontaminated by annotation gaps.
This is the headline result.
EG-NMS weights Thread S detections higher in reliable patches and
Thread Q higher in degraded patches; the under-detection rate drops
from 22.5% (C1) to 19.4% (C2), confirming CAPE recovers annotated
objects without amplifying YOLO hallucinations.
This bias is not specific to CADENet:
any
enhancement method
evaluated on DAWN carries the same one-directional penalty.

VI
Conclusion
We presented
CADENet
, a training-free three-thread system
that separates the safety path (Thread S,
∼
44
{\sim}44
FPS,
zero added latency
) from the quality-enhancement path
(Thread Q: WEM
→
\to
CAPE
→
\to
YOLOv11m
→
\to
EG-NMS) and
a background analytics thread (Thread E: CLIP zero-shot + ResNet50
k
k
-NN), eliminating the deployment conflict of prior single-thread
pipelines.
PEE and EG-NMS weight detections by patch reliability,
suppressing weather-induced false positives
without learned
parameters.
Evaluated on 1,327 DAWN images, CADENet recovers
+
0.0103

Critically, C2 detections
surpass ground truth completeness
:
the enhanced detector reveals real objects invisible through the original
degradation, objects that human annotators could not label—demonstrating
that the true enhancement gain exceeds all reported

We formally characterise this annotation completeness bias, generalizable
to any enhancement method on DAWN-class datasets, and establish recall
as the annotation-gap-immune cross-method comparator.

preview_text:
Adverse weather (rain, fog, sand, and snow) degrades camera-based object detection in autonomous vehicles. Existing enhancement-then-detect approaches stall the safety-critical perception loop, violating hard real-time requirements. Progress on this problem is also constrained by an under-recognized evaluation ceiling: ground truth annotated on degraded images cannot credit a detector that recovers objects the annotators themselves could not see, so a genuinely useful enhancement can register as a near-flat F1 gain. This paper presents CADENet (Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-stream Enhancement Network), a training-free three-thread system: Thread S (YOLOv11n) delivers detections at full frame rate with zero added latency; Thread Q applies condition-adaptive enhancement (CAPE) and fuses results via entropy-guided NMS (EG-NMS) without blocking Thread S; Thread E provides CLIP zero-shot weather classification, so new weather categories require only a new text prompt, with no labeled data and no retraining. Evaluated on 1327 DAWN images (YOLOv11m, IoU = 0.5, confidence = 0.25), CADENet achieves Recall = 0.0103 (micro), F1 = 0.0230 on snow, and F1 = 0.0038 on rain. We formalize the annotation completeness bias on DAWN-class data, so the reported F1 values are lower bounds on the true gain; recall is the annotation-gap-immune headline metric. Thread S sustains approximately 44 FPS regardless of enhancement load. No model retraining or additional sensor hardware is required.

CADENet: Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-Stream Enhancement Network for Adverse Weather Perception in Autonomous Driving
Sherif Khairy
1,2
, and Catherine M. Elias

,
